summary: Julia Evans explores how to build a 'jstack for Ruby' — a tool that can inspect a running Ruby program's stack trace without pre-instrumentation. She walks through using gdb to read Ruby's internal data structures and extract stack frames from memory, then explains why gdb's ptrace-based approach is too heavyweight. She discovers the process_vm_readv system call, which can read another process's memory with minimal impact, and builds a prototype Rust program that samples Ruby stack traces every 10 milliseconds to generate flame graphs.
key_points: Java and C have built-in tools (jstack, perf top) to inspect running programs without pre-instrumentation, but Ruby and Python lack equivalent tooling
gdb can extract Ruby stack traces by traversing internal structs (rb_thread_t, rb_control_frame_t, rb_iseq_t), but uses ptrace which is slow and stops the target process
The process_vm_readv system call can read another process's memory directly without passing through kernel space, with much less impact on the running program
Building a debugger requires two things: debugging info (DWARF) to understand struct layouts, and the ability to read target process memory
She built a working Rust prototype that samples Ruby stack traces every 10ms and generates flame graphs, though it has hardcoded struct types
money_quotes: One of the most frustrating things to me is – when I run a Ruby or Python program, I can't find out what that program is doing RIGHT NOW.
IF JAVA CAN HAVE NICE THINGS THEN WHY NOT RUBY AND PYTHON??
When you attach to a program with gdb, it seems like basically black magic, but as usual it's actually not magic at all.
This is amazing, because we started with practically nothing – just an address of the current thread! And we finished with a file and a place we are in that file.

The reason this is awesome and better than what gdb does is – as far as I can tell, the impact of process_vm_readv on the running process is WAY SMALLER. You just spy on the memory and get out of the way!
Instead of feeling bad that I haven't actually gotten the software to work yet on other peoples' computers, I thought I would take a couple of hours and tell you how it works!
key_insight: You can build lightweight debugging tools for any language runtime by combining DWARF debug info with process_vm_readv to read process memory without stopping the target program.
